AARON: Aaron was the son of Amram and Jochebed of the tribe of Levi, the elder brother of Moses and Miriam, and served as Moses' spokesman before Pharaoh (Exodus 4:14-16; 7:1-2). He was consecrated as the first high priest of Israel, and his rod budded as a token that God had chosen his house for the priesthood (Exodus 28; Numbers 17:8). He sinned in making the golden calf at Sinai and, with Moses, at the waters of Meribah, for which he was not permitted to enter Canaan (Exodus 32; Numbers 20:12). He died on mount Hor at the age of one hundred twenty-three years, and his son Eleazar succeeded him (Numbers 20:28; 33:39).
ABISHAI: Abishai was the son of Zeruiah, David's sister, and brother of Joab and Asahel (1 Chronicles 2:16). He went down with David into Saul's camp by night and would have slain the sleeping king, but David restrained him (1 Samuel 26:6-9). A valiant captain in David's armies, he lifted up his spear against three hundred and slew them, and he rescued David from Ishbi-benob the giant (2 Samuel 21:16-17; 23:18).
ABRAHAM: Abram, son of Terah, was called of God out of Ur of the Chaldees, and God covenanted to make of him a great nation and to bless all families of the earth in him (Genesis 11:31; 12:1-3). His name was changed to Abraham, for God made him a father of many nations; he believed God, and it was counted to him for righteousness (Genesis 17:5; 15:6). In his old age Sarah bare him Isaac, the son of promise, whom Abraham offered upon an altar in obedience to God until the angel of the LORD stayed his hand (Genesis 21:2-3; 22:1-14). He died at one hundred seventy-five years old and was buried in the cave of Machpelah; the scripture calls him the Friend of God (Genesis 25:7-9; James 2:23).
ABSALOM: Absalom was the third son of David, born of Maacah in Hebron, and in all Israel there was none so praised for his beauty (2 Samuel 3:3; 14:25). He slew his brother Amnon for humbling his sister Tamar, fled to Geshur, and after his return stole the hearts of the men of Israel and rose in rebellion against his father (2 Samuel 13:28-29; 15:1-14). Riding through the wood of Ephraim, his head caught hold of an oak, and Joab thrust three darts through his heart; David wept over him, crying, O my son Absalom (2 Samuel 18:9-15, 33).
ADAM: Adam was the first man, formed of the dust of the ground, into whose nostrils God breathed the breath of life, and he was placed in the garden of Eden to dress it and keep it (Genesis 2:7, 15). God made a woman from his rib to be an help meet for him, and Adam gave names to all cattle and every beast of the field (Genesis 2:18-23). He ate of the forbidden tree at his wife's giving, whereby sin entered into the world and death by sin, and he was driven from the garden (Genesis 3:6, 23-24; Romans 5:12). He lived nine hundred and thirty years, and begat sons and daughters, among them Cain, Abel, and Seth (Genesis 5:3-5).
AHAB: Ahab the son of Omri reigned over Israel in Samaria twenty and two years, and did evil in the sight of the LORD above all that were before him (1 Kings 16:29-30). He took Jezebel of Zidon to wife, served Baal, and by her instigation slew Naboth for his vineyard, for which Elijah pronounced judgment upon his house (1 Kings 16:31; 21:1-24). Disguising himself in battle at Ramoth-gilead, he was smitten by a bow drawn at a venture and died at even, and the dogs licked up his blood according to the word of the LORD (1 Kings 22:34-38).
AHAZ: Ahaz the son of Jotham began to reign over Judah at twenty years old, and reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem, but did not that which was right in the sight of the LORD (2 Kings 16:1-2). He made his son to pass through the fire after the abominations of the heathen, hired the king of Assyria against Syria and Israel, and set up a copy of the altar of Damascus in the house of the LORD (2 Kings 16:3, 7-16). To him the LORD gave through Isaiah the sign: Behold, a virgin shall conceive, and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel (Isaiah 7:10-14).
AHAZIAH: Two kings bore this name. Ahaziah the son of Ahab reigned over Israel two years, walked in the ways of his father and mother, and fell through a lattice in Samaria; he sent to inquire of Baal-zebub the god of Ekron, and Elijah declared he should surely die (1 Kings 22:51-53; 2 Kings 1:2-17). Ahaziah the son of Jehoram reigned one year over Judah, walked in the way of the house of Ahab, and was slain by Jehu when he visited Joram at Jezreel (2 Kings 8:25-27; 9:27).
AHIJAH: Several men bore this name, chief among them Ahijah the Shilonite, the prophet who rent his new garment in twelve pieces and gave ten to Jeroboam, foretelling that God would rend the kingdom out of the hand of Solomon (1 Kings 11:29-31). When Jeroboam's wife came to him disguised concerning her sick child, the LORD revealed it to the blind prophet, and he pronounced the destruction of the house of Jeroboam (1 Kings 14:1-16).
ANDREW: Andrew of Bethsaida, brother of Simon Peter, was a disciple of John the Baptist who heard him say, Behold the Lamb of God, and followed Jesus (John 1:35-40). He first findeth his own brother Simon, saying, We have found the Messias, and brought him to Jesus (John 1:41-42). A fisherman called from his nets to become a fisher of men, he was numbered among the twelve apostles, and it was Andrew who told Jesus of the lad with five barley loaves and two small fishes (Matthew 4:18-19; 10:2; John 6:8-9).
ANGEL (Holy Trinity): The angel of the LORD appears throughout the scriptures speaking as God Himself: He appeared to Hagar in the wilderness, to Abraham on Moriah, and to Moses in a flame of fire out of the midst of the bush, saying, I am the God of thy father (Genesis 16:7-13; 22:11-12; Exodus 3:2-6). He went before Israel in the pillar of cloud, and Jacob testified of the Angel which redeemed him from all evil (Exodus 14:19; Genesis 48:16). He appeared to Joshua as captain of the host of the LORD, and to Manoah, who said, We shall surely die, because we have seen God (Joshua 5:13-15; Judges 13:21-22).
ASAPH: Asaph the son of Berachiah, a Levite, was appointed by David as chief of those who ministered before the ark with psalteries, harps, and cymbals, to celebrate, thank, and praise the LORD God of Israel (1 Chronicles 15:17-19; 16:4-5). He was a seer, and twelve psalms bear his name (2 Chronicles 29:30; Psalms 50, 73-83). His sons were set apart to prophesy with harps, and the children of Asaph continued as singers in the temple through the days of Ezra and Nehemiah (1 Chronicles 25:1-2; Ezra 3:10; Nehemiah 12:46).
ASHER: Asher was the eighth son of Jacob, born of Zilpah, Leah's maid, and Leah said, Happy am I, for the daughters will call me blessed (Genesis 30:12-13). Jacob blessed him saying, Out of Asher his bread shall be fat, and he shall yield royal dainties, and Moses said, Let Asher be blessed with children, and let him dip his foot in oil (Genesis 49:20; Deuteronomy 33:24). His tribe received its inheritance along the sea coast in the north of Canaan (Joshua 19:24-31).
AZARIAH: This name was borne by many men of Judah and Levi. Chief among them was Azariah (also called Uzziah) the son of Amaziah, who reigned fifty and two years in Jerusalem and did right in the sight of the LORD, but the LORD smote the king so that he was a leper unto the day of his death (2 Kings 15:1-5). Another Azariah, the companion of Daniel called Abed-nego, refused the king's meat and was delivered from the burning fiery furnace (Daniel 1:6-7; 3:16-27). Azariah the priest, with fourscore priests, withstood king Uzziah when he went in to burn incense in the temple (2 Chronicles 26:17-18).
BALAAM: Balaam the son of Beor, of Pethor by the river, was hired by Balak king of Moab to curse Israel, but God turned the curse into a blessing (Numbers 22:5-6; Deuteronomy 23:4-5). His ass saw the angel of the LORD standing in the way and spake with man's voice, forbidding the madness of the prophet (Numbers 22:22-33; 2 Peter 2:15-16). Though he could not curse Israel, he taught Balak to cast a stumblingblock before them, and he was slain with the sword when Israel warred against Midian (Numbers 31:8, 16; Revelation 2:14).
BARNABAS: Joses, surnamed Barnabas by the apostles (which is, being interpreted, The son of consolation), was a Levite of Cyprus who sold his land and laid the money at the apostles' feet (Acts 4:36-37). He brought Saul to the apostles when the disciples feared him, sought him out at Tarsus to labour with him at Antioch, and was sent forth with him by the Holy Ghost on the first missionary journey (Acts 9:26-27; 11:22-26; 13:2-3). He was a good man, and full of the Holy Ghost and of faith; after sharp contention with Paul concerning John Mark, he took Mark and sailed unto Cyprus (Acts 11:24; 15:36-39).
BENAIAH: Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, of Kabzeel, was a valiant man of many acts: he slew two lionlike men of Moab, went down and slew a lion in the midst of a pit in time of snow, and slew an Egyptian, a goodly man, with his own spear (2 Samuel 23:20-21). He was set over David's guard, remained faithful during Adonijah's usurpation, and Solomon put him in Joab's room over the host (2 Samuel 23:23; 1 Kings 1:8; 2:35).
BENJAMIN: Benjamin was the youngest son of Jacob and the second of Rachel, who died in bearing him and called him Ben-oni, but his father called him Benjamin (Genesis 35:16-18). Jacob would not send him into Egypt with his brethren, saying, His brother is dead, and he alone is left; but Joseph, when he saw him, said, God be gracious unto thee, my son (Genesis 42:38; 43:29). Jacob prophesied, Benjamin shall ravin as a wolf; from his tribe came Saul the first king of Israel, and Saul of Tarsus the apostle (Genesis 49:27; 1 Samuel 9:1-2; Philippians 3:5).
BETH-SHEMESH: Beth-shemesh was a city of Judah given to the sons of Aaron, lying in the valley toward the Philistine border (Joshua 21:16). Thither the kine brought the ark of the LORD on a new cart from the Philistines, and the men of Beth-shemesh were smitten because they had looked into the ark of the LORD (1 Samuel 6:12-19). There also Jehoash king of Israel took Amaziah king of Judah captive in battle (2 Kings 14:11-13).
CALEB: Caleb the son of Jephunneh, of the tribe of Judah, was one of the twelve sent to spy out Canaan, and he stilled the people, saying, Let us go up at once and possess it, for we are well able to overcome it (Numbers 13:6, 30). Because he wholly followed the LORD, he alone with Joshua of that generation entered the land (Numbers 14:24, 30). At fourscore and five years old he was as strong as in the day Moses sent him, and he drove out the three sons of Anak from Hebron, which was given him for an inheritance (Joshua 14:6-14; 15:13-14).
CANAAN: Canaan was the son of Ham and grandson of Noah, upon whom Noah pronounced, Cursed be Canaan; a servant of servants shall he be unto his brethren (Genesis 9:18-25). His descendants peopled the land which bore his name, the land flowing with milk and honey which God sware to give unto Abraham and his seed (Genesis 10:15-19; Exodus 3:8). Israel possessed the land of Canaan under Joshua, when the iniquity of its nations was full (Genesis 15:16; Joshua 11:23).
DANIEL: Daniel, of the children of Judah, was carried captive to Babylon in his youth and purposed in his heart that he would not defile himself with the king's meat (Daniel 1:3-8). God gave him knowledge and understanding in all visions and dreams: he declared and interpreted Nebuchadnezzar's dreams, read the writing on Belshazzar's wall, and received visions of things to come even unto the time of the end (Daniel 1:17; 2; 5; 7-12). Cast into the den of lions for praying to his God, he was delivered, for the LORD sent His angel and shut the lions' mouths; the scripture calls him a man greatly beloved (Daniel 6:16-22; 10:11).
DAVID: David the son of Jesse, of Bethlehem-judah, was a shepherd whom the LORD chose and Samuel anointed king, for the LORD seeth not as man seeth, but looketh on the heart (1 Samuel 16:1-13). He slew Goliath of Gath with a sling and a stone in the name of the LORD of hosts, endured the persecutions of Saul, and reigned forty years over Israel, taking Jerusalem and bringing up the ark (1 Samuel 17; 2 Samuel 5-6). He sinned grievously in the matter of Uriah's wife but repented at Nathan's rebuke; the sweet psalmist of Israel, he wrote many psalms, and God covenanted that his throne should be established for ever, a promise fulfilled in Christ, the son of David (2 Samuel 11-12; 23:1; 7:12-16; Matthew 1:1).
DEBIR: Debir, earlier called Kirjath-sepher, was a city in the hill country of Judah whose king was slain by Joshua (Joshua 10:38-39; 12:13). Caleb said, He that smiteth Kirjath-sepher, and taketh it, to him will I give Achsah my daughter to wife; and Othniel the son of Kenaz took it (Joshua 15:15-17). It was afterward given to the priests, the sons of Aaron (Joshua 21:15). A king of Eglon named Debir was also among the five kings slain by Joshua at Makkedah (Joshua 10:3, 22-26).
DEER: The scriptures speak of the hart, the roebuck, and the fallow deer as clean beasts which Israel might eat (Deuteronomy 12:15; 14:5); they were furnished daily for Solomon's table (1 Kings 4:23). The psalmist sang, As the hart panteth after the water brooks, so panteth my soul after thee, O God (Psalm 42:1). Naphtali is a hind let loose, said Jacob; and the prophet promised, Then shall the lame man leap as an hart (Genesis 49:21; Isaiah 35:6; Habakkuk 3:19).
DIBON: Dibon was a city east of Jordan taken from Sihon king of the Amorites, built by the children of Gad, and given to the tribe of Reuben (Numbers 21:30; 32:34; Joshua 13:15-17). It appears in the burden of Moab pronounced by the prophets: he that dwelleth in Dibon is called to come down from glory and sit in thirst, and judgment is come upon her (Isaiah 15:2; Jeremiah 48:18, 22).
EDOMITES: The Edomites were the descendants of Esau, who is Edom, dwelling in mount Seir (Genesis 36:8-9). They refused Israel passage through their border in the wilderness, yet Israel was commanded, Thou shalt not abhor an Edomite; for he is thy brother (Numbers 20:14-21; Deuteronomy 23:7). They were subdued by Saul and David, revolted in later days, and the prophets pronounced judgment upon Edom for his violence against his brother Jacob (1 Samuel 14:47; 2 Samuel 8:14; 2 Kings 8:20; Obadiah 1:10).
ELEAZAR (ELEAZER): Eleazar, the third son of Aaron, was consecrated a priest and succeeded his father as high priest when Aaron died on mount Hor (Exodus 28:1; Numbers 20:25-28). He stood with Moses at the numbering of Israel and with Joshua divided the land of Canaan by lot for an inheritance (Numbers 26:1-3; Joshua 14:1). He died and was buried in a hill that pertained to Phinehas his son (Joshua 24:33). Others of this name include Eleazar the son of Abinadab, sanctified to keep the ark, and Eleazar the son of Dodo, one of David's three mighty men (1 Samuel 7:1; 2 Samuel 23:9-10).
ELI: Eli was priest of the LORD in Shiloh and judged Israel forty years; unto him Hannah brought the child Samuel, whom she had lent to the LORD (1 Samuel 1:9-28; 4:18). His sons Hophni and Phinehas were sons of Belial who knew not the LORD, and because Eli restrained them not, judgment was pronounced upon his house (1 Samuel 2:12, 22-36; 3:11-14). When word came that the ark of God was taken and his two sons slain, Eli fell backward from his seat, and his neck brake, and he died (1 Samuel 4:12-18).
ELIJAH: Elijah the Tishbite, of the inhabitants of Gilead, declared unto Ahab that there should be neither dew nor rain but according to his word, and was fed by ravens at the brook Cherith and by a widow at Zarephath whose barrel of meal wasted not (1 Kings 17:1-16). On mount Carmel he called down fire from heaven before all Israel, that they might know that the LORD, He is God, and slew the prophets of Baal (1 Kings 18:17-40). He was taken up by a whirlwind into heaven in a chariot of fire, and appeared with Moses on the mount of transfiguration talking with Jesus (2 Kings 2:11; Matthew 17:3).
ELISHA: Elisha the son of Shaphat was ploughing with twelve yoke of oxen when Elijah cast his mantle upon him, and he ministered unto Elijah until he received a double portion of his spirit (1 Kings 19:19-21; 2 Kings 2:9-15). He wrought many miracles: he healed the waters of Jericho, multiplied the widow's oil, raised the Shunammite's son, healed Naaman the leper, and made the iron to swim (2 Kings 2-6). Even after his death, a dead man let down into his sepulchre revived and stood up when he touched the bones of Elisha (2 Kings 13:20-21).
ESAU: Esau, the firstborn of Isaac and twin brother of Jacob, was a cunning hunter, a man of the field, red and hairy (Genesis 25:24-27). Being faint from the field, he sold his birthright to Jacob for bread and pottage of lentiles, and afterward lost the blessing also, crying with a great and exceeding bitter cry (Genesis 25:29-34; 27:34-38). Though he purposed to slay his brother, when Jacob returned from Padan-aram Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and they wept (Genesis 27:41; 33:4). He dwelt in mount Seir, and is the father of the Edomites (Genesis 36:8-9; Hebrews 12:16-17).
EZEKIEL: Ezekiel the priest, the son of Buzi, was among the captives by the river of Chebar in the land of the Chaldeans when the heavens were opened and he saw visions of God (Ezekiel 1:1-3). The LORD made him a watchman unto the house of Israel, and he prophesied by visions, parables, and signs, bearing the iniquity of the house of Israel in his own body (Ezekiel 3:17; 4-5). He saw the valley of dry bones live again, and the vision of a new temple with the glory of the LORD returning, and the city whose name is The LORD is there (Ezekiel 37; 40-48).
EZRA: Ezra the priest, the son of Seraiah, was a ready scribe in the law of Moses, and the hand of the LORD his God was upon him (Ezra 7:1-6). He went up from Babylon to Jerusalem in the reign of Artaxerxes, for he had prepared his heart to seek the law of the LORD, and to do it, and to teach in Israel statutes and judgments (Ezra 7:10). He mourned over the mingling of the holy seed with the people of the lands and led the people in confession, and he read the book of the law before the congregation from morning until midday (Ezra 9-10; Nehemiah 8:1-8).
GATH: Gath was one of the five chief cities of the Philistines, the home of Goliath the giant whom David slew (Joshua 13:3; 1 Samuel 17:4). The ark of God was carried thither, and the hand of the LORD was against the city (1 Samuel 5:8-9). David twice fled to Gath from Saul, feigning madness before Achish the first time and afterward dwelling in the country of the Philistines; David lamented over Saul, Tell it not in Gath (1 Samuel 21:10-15; 27:2-4; 2 Samuel 1:20).
GAZA: Gaza was a city of the Philistines in the south, one of their five lordly cities, allotted to Judah but held by the Philistines (Joshua 15:47; Judges 1:18; 1 Samuel 6:17). Samson carried away its gates by night, and there, blinded and bound, he pulled down the house upon the lords of the Philistines, slaying more at his death than in his life (Judges 16:1-3, 21-30). The prophets pronounced judgment upon Gaza, and on the road from Jerusalem to Gaza Philip preached Jesus to the Ethiopian eunuch (Amos 1:6-7; Zephaniah 2:4; Acts 8:26-39).
GEZER: Gezer was a Canaanite city whose king Horam was slain by Joshua, and it was given to the Kohathite Levites out of Ephraim (Joshua 10:33; 21:21). Ephraim drave not out the Canaanites that dwelt in Gezer, but they served under tribute (Joshua 16:10; Judges 1:29). Pharaoh king of Egypt took Gezer, burnt it with fire, and gave it for a present unto his daughter, Solomon's wife, and Solomon built it (1 Kings 9:16-17).
GIDEON: Gideon the son of Joash, of Manasseh, was threshing wheat by the winepress to hide it from the Midianites when the angel of the LORD appeared, saying, The LORD is with thee, thou mighty man of valour (Judges 6:11-12). He threw down the altar of Baal, was called Jerubbaal, and asked signs of God with the fleece of wool (Judges 6:25-40). With three hundred men bearing trumpets, pitchers, and lamps, crying, The sword of the LORD, and of Gideon, he routed the host of Midian (Judges 7). He refused to rule over Israel, saying, The LORD shall rule over you, and the country was in quietness forty years in his days (Judges 8:22-23, 28).
HAMATH: Hamath was a city and kingdom in the north, and the entrance of Hamath marked the northern border of Israel's inheritance (Numbers 34:8; Joshua 13:5). Toi king of Hamath sent his son to salute and bless David when he had smitten Hadadezer (2 Samuel 8:9-10). Solomon built store cities in Hamath, and Jeroboam the son of Joash recovered Hamath for Israel; from thence also the king of Assyria brought men to dwell in Samaria (2 Chronicles 8:4; 2 Kings 14:28; 17:24).
HANAN: Hanan was a name borne by several men: a Benjamite of the sons of Shashak, a son of Azel of the house of Saul, one of David's valiant men, and heads of families among the Nethinims who returned from captivity (1 Chronicles 8:23, 38; 11:43; Ezra 2:46). Levites of this name caused the people to understand the law when Ezra read it, and sealed unto the covenant (Nehemiah 8:7; 10:10). The sons of Hanan the son of Igdaliah, a man of God, had a chamber in the house of the LORD in Jeremiah's day (Jeremiah 35:4).
HANANI: Hanani was the name of several men. Hanani the seer rebuked Asa king of Judah for relying on the king of Syria and not on the LORD, and was put in a prison house (2 Chronicles 16:7-10). Another Hanani, brother of Nehemiah, brought him word of the affliction of Jerusalem, and was afterward given charge over the city, for he was a faithful man, and feared God above many (Nehemiah 1:2; 7:2). A son of Heman of this name served in song, and a priest Hanani was among the musicians at the dedication of the wall (1 Chronicles 25:4, 25; Nehemiah 12:36).
HANANIAH: Hananiah was a common name in Israel. Hananiah the son of Azur, the false prophet of Gibeon, broke the yoke from Jeremiah's neck and prophesied lies, and died the same year according to the word of the LORD (Jeremiah 28). Another Hananiah was the companion of Daniel called Shadrach, delivered from the fiery furnace (Daniel 1:6-7; 3). Others include a son of Heman the singer, a captain of Uzziah, the father of a prince in Jeremiah's day, a son of Zerubbabel, and the ruler of the palace under Nehemiah, a faithful man who feared God (1 Chronicles 25:4; 2 Chronicles 26:11; Jeremiah 36:12; 1 Chronicles 3:19; Nehemiah 7:2).
HARAN: Haran the son of Terah was the brother of Abram and Nahor and the father of Lot, Milcah, and Iscah; he died before his father Terah in Ur of the Chaldees (Genesis 11:26-28). His name was also borne by the city of Mesopotamia where Terah settled and died, whence Abram departed at God's call, and whither Jacob fled to Laban (Genesis 11:31-32; 12:4-5; 27:43). A Levite and a son of Caleb also bore this name (1 Chronicles 23:9; 2:46).
HASHABIAH: Hashabiah was a name borne by many Levites: sons of Merari, musicians of the sons of Jeduthun, rulers over Israel for the business of the LORD, and chiefs who returned from the captivity (1 Chronicles 6:45; 25:3, 19; 26:30; 27:17). Ezra called for ministers for the house of God, and Hashabiah, a chief of the priests, was among those to whom the silver and gold were weighed (Ezra 8:19, 24). One Hashabiah, ruler of the half part of Keilah, repaired a portion of the wall of Jerusalem, and Levites of this name sealed the covenant (Nehemiah 3:17; 10:11).
HEROD: The scriptures speak of several rulers named Herod. Herod the king, in the days of Jesus' birth, demanded of the wise men where Christ should be born, and slew all the children in Bethlehem from two years old and under (Matthew 2:1-16). Herod the tetrarch beheaded John the Baptist for Herodias' sake, and set Jesus at nought when Pilate sent Him (Matthew 14:1-11; Luke 23:7-11). Another Herod the king killed James the brother of John with the sword and imprisoned Peter; he was smitten by the angel of the LORD, because he gave not God the glory, and was eaten of worms (Acts 12:1-4, 21-23).
HEZEKIAH: Hezekiah the son of Ahaz reigned twenty and nine years in Jerusalem, and he trusted in the LORD God of Israel, so that after him was none like him among all the kings of Judah (2 Kings 18:1-5). He removed the high places, brake the brazen serpent that Moses had made, and restored the worship of the house of the LORD (2 Kings 18:4; 2 Chronicles 29-31). When Sennacherib king of Assyria came against Jerusalem, Hezekiah spread the letter before the LORD, and the angel of the LORD smote in the camp of the Assyrians an hundred fourscore and five thousand (2 Kings 19:14-35). Being sick unto death, he prayed, and the LORD added fifteen years to his days, bringing the shadow ten degrees backward for a sign (2 Kings 20:1-11).
HOSHEA: Hoshea the son of Elah slew Pekah and reigned in his stead, the last king of Israel, and he did evil in the sight of the LORD, but not as the kings of Israel that were before him (2 Kings 15:30; 17:1-2). He became servant to Shalmaneser king of Assyria, then conspired with So king of Egypt; the king of Assyria shut him up in prison, besieged Samaria three years, and carried Israel away into Assyria, because they sinned against the LORD their God (2 Kings 17:3-7). Hoshea was also the earlier name of Joshua the son of Nun, whom Moses called Jehoshua (Numbers 13:8, 16; Deuteronomy 32:44).
HUR: Hur, with Aaron, stayed up the hands of Moses on the top of the hill until the going down of the sun, and Amalek was discomfited (Exodus 17:10-13). He was left with Aaron in charge of the people when Moses went up into mount Sinai (Exodus 24:14). Of the tribe of Judah, he was the grandfather of Bezaleel, whom God filled with wisdom for the work of the tabernacle (Exodus 31:2-5; 1 Chronicles 2:19-20). A king of Midian named Hur was also slain by Israel with Balaam (Numbers 31:8).
ISAAC: Isaac, the son of promise, was born to Abraham and Sarah in their old age, and Sarah said, God hath made me to laugh (Genesis 21:1-6). Abraham offered him upon the altar at Moriah, and he received Rebekah to wife, whom he loved (Genesis 22; 24:67). The LORD appeared to him and confirmed the covenant of Abraham; he sowed and received an hundredfold, digged again the wells of his father, and blessed Jacob and Esau concerning things to come (Genesis 26:1-5, 12-25; 27; Hebrews 11:20). He died at an hundred and fourscore years, and his sons Esau and Jacob buried him (Genesis 35:28-29).
ISAIAH: Isaiah the son of Amoz prophesied concerning Judah and Jerusalem in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah (Isaiah 1:1). In the year that king Uzziah died he saw the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up, and a seraphim laid a live coal upon his mouth, and he answered, Here am I; send me (Isaiah 6:1-8). He counseled kings, foretold the virgin's Son called Immanuel and the Child whose name is Wonderful, Counsellor, The mighty God, and wrote of the suffering Servant wounded for our transgressions (Isaiah 7:14; 9:6; 53). He walked naked and barefoot three years for a sign, and by his word the LORD delivered Jerusalem from Sennacherib and healed Hezekiah (Isaiah 20:2-3; 37-38).
ISHMAEL: Ishmael was the son of Abram by Hagar, Sarai's Egyptian handmaid; the angel of the LORD foretold he would be a wild man, his hand against every man (Genesis 16:11-12). He was circumcised at thirteen years, and when he was cast out with his mother, God heard the voice of the lad in the wilderness and promised to make him a great nation (Genesis 17:25; 21:9-21). He begat twelve princes and died at an hundred and thirty and seven years (Genesis 25:12-17). Another Ishmael, the son of Nethaniah, of the seed royal, treacherously slew Gedaliah the governor after the fall of Jerusalem (2 Kings 25:25; Jeremiah 41).
ISSACHAR: Issachar was the ninth son of Jacob, the fifth of Leah, who said, God hath given me my hire (Genesis 30:17-18). Jacob prophesied, Issachar is a strong ass couching down between two burdens, bowing his shoulder to bear (Genesis 49:14-15). His tribe's inheritance lay in the goodly land toward Jezreel, and of the children of Issachar were men that had understanding of the times, to know what Israel ought to do (Joshua 19:17-23; 1 Chronicles 12:32).
JACOB: Jacob, the younger twin of Esau, took hold of his brother's heel at birth, purchased the birthright, and by subtilty obtained the blessing of Isaac (Genesis 25:26-34; 27). Fleeing to Padan-aram, he saw at Bethel a ladder reaching to heaven, served Laban for Rachel and Leah, and returning, wrestled with a man until the breaking of the day, who said, Thy name shall be called no more Jacob, but Israel: for as a prince hast thou power with God and with men (Genesis 28:12-15; 29; 32:24-28). His twelve sons became the tribes of Israel; he went down into Egypt to Joseph, blessed the sons of Joseph and his own sons, and died there, aged an hundred forty and seven years, and was buried in Machpelah (Genesis 46-50).
JAMES: Two apostles bore this name: James the son of Zebedee, brother of John, called from mending nets, was of the three nearest to Jesus at the raising of Jairus' daughter, on the mount of transfiguration, and in Gethsemane, and was killed with the sword by Herod (Matthew 4:21-22; 17:1; 26:37; Acts 12:2). James the son of Alphaeus was also of the twelve (Matthew 10:3). James the Lord's brother saw the risen Christ, spoke as a pillar at the Jerusalem council, and wrote the epistle bearing his name to the twelve tribes scattered abroad (1 Corinthians 15:7; Acts 15:13-21; Galatians 2:9; James 1:1).
JEBUSITES: The Jebusites, descended from Canaan, dwelt in the mountains at Jebus, which is Jerusalem (Genesis 10:15-16; Numbers 13:29; Judges 19:10). The children of Judah and Benjamin could not drive them out, and they dwelt with the children of Israel unto this day (Joshua 15:63; Judges 1:21). David took the strong hold of Zion from them, and afterward bought the threshingfloor of Araunah the Jebusite, where the temple was built (2 Samuel 5:6-9; 24:16-25; 2 Chronicles 3:1).
JEHIEL: Jehiel was a name borne by many Levites and men of Israel: a musician appointed by David before the ark, a Gershonite ruler over the treasures of the house of the LORD, a son of Hachmoni who was with the king's sons, and one who was with the sons of Jehoshaphat (1 Chronicles 15:18; 23:8; 29:8; 27:32; 2 Chronicles 21:2). Others of this name served in the cleansing of the temple under Hezekiah, were rulers of the house of God in Josiah's day, and appear among those who returned with Ezra and who had taken strange wives (2 Chronicles 29:14; 35:8; Ezra 8:9; 10:26).
JEHOAHAZ: Two kings bore this name. Jehoahaz the son of Jehu reigned seventeen years over Israel and did evil, and the LORD delivered Israel into the hand of Hazael king of Syria; but Jehoahaz besought the LORD, and the LORD hearkened and gave Israel a saviour (2 Kings 13:1-5). Jehoahaz the son of Josiah was made king of Judah by the people, reigned three months, and was put in bands by Pharaoh-nechoh and carried to Egypt, where he died (2 Kings 23:30-34).
JEHOHANAN: Jehohanan was the name of several men: a Korhite porter of the house of the LORD, a captain of hundreds of Judah under Jehoshaphat, and the father of Ishmael, a captain of hundreds who helped Jehoiada set Joash on the throne (1 Chronicles 26:3; 2 Chronicles 17:15; 23:1). After the captivity, an Ezra chamber-holder's son, a priest of the days of Joiakim, and a singer at the dedication of the wall bore this name (Ezra 10:6; Nehemiah 12:13, 42).
JEHOIACHIN: Jehoiachin the son of Jehoiakim was eighteen years old when he began to reign, and he reigned in Jerusalem three months, and did evil in the sight of the LORD (2 Kings 24:8-9). He went out to the king of Babylon and was carried away captive with his mothers, his servants, and the mighty of the land (2 Kings 24:12-16). In the seven and thirtieth year of his captivity, Evil-merodach king of Babylon lifted up his head out of prison, spake kindly to him, and set his throne above the thrones of the kings that were with him in Babylon (2 Kings 25:27-30).
JEHOIADA: Jehoiada the priest hid Joash the son of Ahaziah six years in the house of the LORD from Athaliah, then brought forth the king's son, crowned him, and made a covenant between the LORD and the king and the people (2 Kings 11:1-17). All the days of Jehoiada, Joash did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, and Jehoiada repaired the house of the LORD (2 Kings 12:2; 2 Chronicles 24:4-14). He died an hundred and thirty years old, and they buried him in the city of David among the kings, because he had done good in Israel, both toward God, and toward his house (2 Chronicles 24:15-16).
JEHORAM: Two kings bore this name (also written Joram). Jehoram the son of Jehoshaphat, king of Judah, slew all his brethren with the sword, walked in the way of the kings of Israel, having the daughter of Ahab to wife, and departed without being desired, dying of an incurable disease of his bowels as Elijah had written (2 Chronicles 21). Jehoram the son of Ahab reigned over Israel twelve years, wrought evil, and was slain by Jehu with an arrow through his heart and cast into the portion of the field of Naboth (2 Kings 3:1-2; 9:24-26).
JEIEL: Jeiel was the name of several men: a Reubenite chief, a Levite musician who played with psalteries before the ark, an ancestor of the house of Saul in Gibeon, and a scribe under king Uzziah (1 Chronicles 5:7; 15:18, 21; 9:35; 2 Chronicles 26:11). A Levite of the sons of Asaph of this name stood in the congregation when the Spirit came upon Jahaziel in the days of Jehoshaphat, and others appear among the temple ministers of Josiah's passover and those who returned with Ezra (2 Chronicles 20:14; 35:9; Ezra 8:13; 10:43).
JEREMIAH: Jeremiah the son of Hilkiah, of the priests in Anathoth, was ordained a prophet unto the nations before he came forth out of the womb (Jeremiah 1:1-5). Through the reigns of Josiah unto the carrying away of Jerusalem he warned Judah of the Babylonian captivity, and for the word of the LORD he was smitten, put in the stocks, imprisoned, and cast into a miry dungeon (Jeremiah 1:2-3; 20:2; 37-38). He prophesied the seventy years' captivity and the new covenant, when God would write His law in the hearts of His people; he wrote also the Lamentations over the desolate city he had wept for, saying, Mine eye runneth down with rivers of water (Jeremiah 25:11; 31:31-34; Lamentations 3:48).
JEROHAM: Jeroham was the name of several men, among them the father of Elkanah and grandfather of Samuel the prophet, a Levite of the sons of Kohath (1 Samuel 1:1; 1 Chronicles 6:27, 34). Benjamites, priests, a Danite prince, and men whose sons dwelt at Jerusalem also bore the name, as did the father of Azariah, a captain who helped Jehoiada crown Joash (1 Chronicles 8:27; 9:8, 12; 12:7; 27:22; 2 Chronicles 23:1).
JESAIAH: Jesaiah (also written Jeshaiah) was borne by several men: a son of Jeduthun who prophesied with a harp in the service of song, a Levite over the treasures dedicated to the LORD, and a grandson of Zerubbabel of the royal line (1 Chronicles 25:3, 15; 26:25; 3:21). Men of this name also returned from Babylon with Ezra, of the sons of Elam and of the sons of Merari, and a Benjamite of this name is named among the fathers of them that dwelt at Jerusalem (Ezra 8:7, 19; Nehemiah 11:7).
JESHUA: Jeshua the son of Jozadak was the high priest who came up from Babylon with Zerubbabel, builded the altar of the God of Israel, and helped lay the foundation of the temple (Ezra 2:2; 3:2-9; 5:2). This Jeshua, called Joshua in the prophets, was shown to Zechariah standing before the angel of the LORD in filthy garments, Satan at his right hand to resist him, and the LORD clothed him with change of raiment, a brand plucked out of the fire (Zechariah 3:1-5; Haggai 1:1). The name was also borne by priests and Levites of the courses of David and of the return, and Jeshua is the form given to Joshua the son of Nun in Nehemiah (1 Chronicles 24:11; Ezra 2:40; Nehemiah 8:17).
JESSE: Jesse the Bethlehemite, son of Obed and grandson of Boaz and Ruth, was the father of eight sons, of whom David was the youngest (Ruth 4:17-22; 1 Samuel 16:10-11; 17:12). Samuel came to Bethlehem and sanctified Jesse and his sons to the sacrifice, and anointed David in the midst of his brethren (1 Samuel 16:1-13). Isaiah prophesied, There shall come forth a rod out of the stem of Jesse, and a Branch shall grow out of his roots; and in that day there shall be a root of Jesse, to which the Gentiles shall seek (Isaiah 11:1, 10).
JEZEBEL: Jezebel, the daughter of Ethbaal king of the Zidonians, became the wife of Ahab king of Israel and stirred him up to serve Baal; she slew the prophets of the LORD and fed the prophets of Baal at her table (1 Kings 16:31; 18:4, 13, 19). She sought Elijah's life after Carmel, and by forged letters and false witnesses procured the death of Naboth for his vineyard (1 Kings 19:1-2; 21:5-16). At Jehu's word she was thrown down from a window in Jezreel, and the dogs ate her flesh according to the word of the LORD by Elijah (2 Kings 9:30-37).
JOAB: Joab the son of Zeruiah, David's sister, was captain of the host of Israel, having gone up first against the Jebusites at David's word (1 Chronicles 11:6; 2 Samuel 8:16). A mighty and subtle man of war, he slew Abner and Amasa treacherously, carried out the king's letter concerning Uriah the Hittite, and thrust three darts through the heart of Absalom against the king's charge (2 Samuel 3:27; 20:9-10; 11:14-17; 18:14). He followed Adonijah, though he had not turned after Absalom, and by Solomon's command was slain at the horns of the altar, according to David's dying charge (1 Kings 1:7; 2:28-34).
JOASH: Joash (also written Jehoash) the son of Ahaziah was hid from Athaliah six years and made king of Judah at seven years old by Jehoiada the priest (2 Kings 11). He repaired the house of the LORD, but after Jehoiada's death he hearkened to the princes, served idols, and stoned Zechariah the son of Jehoiada in the court of the LORD's house; his own servants slew him on his bed (2 Kings 12; 2 Chronicles 24:17-25). Another Joash, the son of Jehoahaz, reigned over Israel, wept over the dying Elisha, and smote Syria thrice as the prophet had said (2 Kings 13:10-25). The father of Gideon also bore this name (Judges 6:11).
JOB: Job was a man in the land of Uz, perfect and upright, one that feared God and eschewed evil, the greatest of all the men of the east (Job 1:1-3). The LORD permitted Satan to take his substance, his ten children, and his health, yet Job sinned not, saying, The LORD gave, and the LORD hath taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD (Job 1:6-22; 2:1-10). Through long dispute with his three friends he held fast his integrity, testifying, I know that my redeemer liveth; and when the LORD answered him out of the whirlwind, he abhorred himself and repented in dust and ashes (Job 19:25; 42:1-6). The LORD turned his captivity and gave him twice as much as he had before, and Job died, being old and full of days (Job 42:10-17).
JOEL: Joel the son of Pethuel prophesied the word of the LORD, declaring a terrible plague of locusts and calling Judah to fasting and repentance: Rend your heart, and not your garments (Joel 1:1-14; 2:12-13). He foretold the pouring out of God's Spirit upon all flesh, which Peter declared fulfilled at Pentecost, and the great and terrible day of the LORD in the valley of decision (Joel 2:28-32; Acts 2:16-21; Joel 3:14). The name was also borne by the firstborn son of Samuel, whose ways were corrupt, and by various Levites, chiefs, and mighty men (1 Samuel 8:1-3; 1 Chronicles 5:4; 11:38; 15:7; 27:20).
JOHN: John the apostle, son of Zebedee and brother of James, was a fisherman called by Jesus, one of the three closest to the Lord, called with his brother Boanerges, the sons of thunder (Matthew 4:21-22; Mark 3:17; 9:2). He is the disciple whom Jesus loved, who leaned on His breast at supper, stood by the cross where Jesus committed His mother to him, and outran Peter to the sepulchre (John 13:23; 19:26-27; 20:2-8). He wrote the gospel and epistles bearing his name, and, in the isle called Patmos for the word of God, received the Revelation of Jesus Christ (John 21:24; 1 John 1:1-4; Revelation 1:1-9). John the Baptist, son of Zacharias, was the voice crying in the wilderness who baptized Jesus and said, Behold the Lamb of God; Jesus said none greater had risen among them born of women (Matthew 3; John 1:29; Matthew 11:11).
JONAH: Jonah the son of Amittai was commanded to cry against Nineveh, but rose up to flee unto Tarshish from the presence of the LORD (Jonah 1:1-3). The LORD sent a great wind, and Jonah was cast into the sea and swallowed by a great fish, in whose belly he prayed three days and three nights (Jonah 1:4-17; 2). Preaching in Nineveh, he saw the city repent at his cry, and God spared it, whereat Jonah was displeased, and the LORD taught him by a gourd His pity for the great city (Jonah 3-4). Jesus gave the sign of the prophet Jonas: as Jonah was three days and three nights in the whale's belly, so should the Son of man be in the heart of the earth (Matthew 12:39-41).
JONATHAN: Jonathan the son of Saul was a valiant prince who, with his armourbearer only, smote the garrison of the Philistines, saying, There is no restraint to the LORD to save by many or by few (1 Samuel 14:1-15). His soul was knit with the soul of David, and he loved him as his own soul, making a covenant with him and strengthening his hand in God against his father's wrath (1 Samuel 18:1-4; 19:1-7; 20; 23:16-18). He fell with Saul on mount Gilboa, and David lamented, Thy love to me was wonderful, passing the love of women (1 Samuel 31:2; 2 Samuel 1:25-26).
JORAM: Joram (also written Jehoram) the son of Ahab reigned over Israel twelve years and wrought evil, though he put away the image of Baal that his father had made (2 Kings 3:1-3). In his days Elisha prophesied, Naaman was healed, and Samaria was besieged and delivered (2 Kings 3-7). Returning wounded from Ramoth-gilead to Jezreel, he was slain by an arrow from the bow of Jehu and cast into the field of Naboth (2 Kings 8:28-29; 9:14-26). Joram the son of Jehoshaphat, king of Judah, bore the same name, as did a son of Toi king of Hamath sent to salute David (2 Kings 8:16-24; 2 Samuel 8:10).
JOSEPH: Joseph, the elder son of Rachel, was loved of Jacob above all his children, who made him a coat of many colours; his brethren, envying his dreams, sold him into Egypt (Genesis 37). In Potiphar's house and in prison the LORD was with him and made all he did to prosper; refusing his master's wife, he said, How then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God? (Genesis 39). Interpreting Pharaoh's dreams, he was set over all the land of Egypt at thirty years old, laid up corn against the famine, and preserved his father's house, saying to his brethren, Ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good (Genesis 41; 45; 50:20). Joseph the carpenter of Nazareth, a just man, was the husband of Mary the mother of Jesus (Matthew 1:16-25); Joseph of Arimathaea, a rich disciple, buried the body of Jesus in his own new tomb (Matthew 27:57-60).
JOSHUA: Joshua the son of Nun, of Ephraim, ministered to Moses from his youth, fought against Amalek at Rephidim, and was one of the two faithful spies who wholly followed the LORD (Exodus 17:9-13; 24:13; Numbers 14:6-9, 38). Full of the spirit of wisdom, he was ordained to succeed Moses, and the LORD charged him, Be strong and of a good courage; this book of the law shall not depart out of thy mouth (Deuteronomy 34:9; Joshua 1:6-9). He led Israel over Jordan on dry ground, took Jericho when the walls fell flat, commanded the sun to stand still, subdued the land, and divided it for an inheritance (Joshua 3-4; 6; 10:12-14; 11:23). At an hundred and ten years old he died, having testified, As for me and my house, we will serve the LORD (Joshua 24:15, 29).
JOSIAH: Josiah the son of Amon was eight years old when he began to reign over Judah, and he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, walking in all the way of David his father, and turning not aside to the right hand or to the left (2 Kings 22:1-2). In his days Hilkiah found the book of the law in the house of the LORD, and Josiah rent his clothes, made a covenant before the LORD, purged Judah and Israel of idolatry, and kept a passover such as had not been holden since the days of the judges (2 Kings 22-23; 2 Chronicles 34-35). His coming and his deeds at Beth-el were foretold by name centuries before (1 Kings 13:2; 2 Kings 23:15-18). He was slain at Megiddo by Pharaoh-nechoh, and Jeremiah lamented for him; like unto him was there no king before him that turned to the LORD with all his heart (2 Kings 23:25-29; 2 Chronicles 35:20-25).
JOTHAM: Jotham the son of Uzziah judged the people while his father was a leper, then reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem, and did that which was right in the sight of the LORD (2 Kings 15:5, 32-34). He built the higher gate of the house of the LORD and prevailed against the Ammonites, and became mighty, because he prepared his ways before the LORD his God (2 Chronicles 27). An earlier Jotham, youngest son of Gideon, escaped the slaughter of his brethren and spoke the parable of the trees from mount Gerizim against Abimelech (Judges 9:5-21).
JUDAH: Judah, the fourth son of Jacob by Leah, counseled his brethren to sell Joseph rather than slay him, and afterward offered himself surety for Benjamin, pleading before Joseph in Egypt (Genesis 29:35; 37:26-27; 44:18-34). Jacob blessed him above his brethren: Judah is a lion's whelp; the sceptre shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh come (Genesis 49:8-12). From his line by Tamar came Pharez, and of Judah came David the king, and the Lord Jesus, the Lion of the tribe of Juda (Genesis 38; Ruth 4:18-22; Matthew 1:2-16; Revelation 5:5). His tribe led the encampments and the conquest, and the southern kingdom bore his name (Numbers 2:3; Judges 1:1-2).
JUDAS (JUDE): Judas Iscariot, one of the twelve, was a thief who bare the bag, and he covenanted with the chief priests for thirty pieces of silver and betrayed the Son of man with a kiss (John 12:6; Matthew 26:14-16, 47-50). Jesus called him the son of perdition, and said, Good were it for that man if he had never been born; repenting himself, he cast down the silver in the temple and went and hanged himself (John 17:12; Mark 14:21; Matthew 27:3-5). Judas the brother of James, also called Lebbaeus Thaddaeus, was another of the twelve, who asked the Lord at supper, How is it that thou wilt manifest thyself unto us, and not unto the world? (Luke 6:16; Matthew 10:3; John 14:22). Jude, servant of Jesus Christ and brother of James, wrote the epistle exhorting believers to earnestly contend for the faith once delivered unto the saints (Jude 1:1-3).
JUDEA: Judaea was the southern province of the land in the days of the Roman rule, wherein Jesus was born at Bethlehem in the days of Herod the king (Matthew 2:1, 5-6). John the Baptist preached in the wilderness of Judaea, and all Judaea went out to him; Jesus taught, healed, and was crucified there, and warned, When ye shall see the abomination of desolation, then let them which be in Judaea flee into the mountains (Matthew 3:1-5; 24:15-16). The witness of the risen Christ was to begin at Jerusalem, in all Judaea, and in Samaria, and unto the uttermost part of the earth, and the churches of Judaea had rest and were edified (Acts 1:8; 9:31).
KEILAH: Keilah was a city of Judah in the lowland which the Philistines fought against and robbed of its threshingfloors (Joshua 15:44; 1 Samuel 23:1). David inquired of the LORD, went down, smote the Philistines, and saved the inhabitants of Keilah (1 Samuel 23:2-5). When Saul sought to come down, David inquired by the ephod whether the men of Keilah would deliver him up, and the LORD said, They will deliver thee up; so David and his men departed (1 Samuel 23:7-13). Rulers of part of Keilah repaired the wall of Jerusalem in Nehemiah's day (Nehemiah 3:17-18).
KIDRON: The brook Kidron lay between Jerusalem and the mount of Olives; David passed over it weeping when he fled from Absalom (2 Samuel 15:23, 30). There reforming kings burnt and cast the idols and abominations of Judah: Asa burnt his mother's idol there, and Hezekiah and Josiah cast the uncleanness of the temple into it (1 Kings 15:13; 2 Chronicles 29:16; 2 Kings 23:4-12). Jesus went forth with His disciples over the brook Cedron into the garden where He was betrayed (John 18:1-2).
KIRJATH-JEARIM: Kirjath-jearim, also called Baalah and Kirjath-baal, was a city of the Gibeonites reckoned to Judah on the border of Benjamin (Joshua 9:17; 15:9, 60; 18:14). When the ark of the LORD was returned by the Philistines, the men of Kirjath-jearim fetched it to the house of Abinadab in the hill, where it abode twenty years, and thence David brought it up toward Jerusalem (1 Samuel 6:21-7:2; 1 Chronicles 13:5-6). Urijah the prophet, who prophesied against Jerusalem in the days of Jehoiakim, was of Kirjath-jearim (Jeremiah 26:20).
LABAN: Laban the Syrian, son of Bethuel and brother of Rebekah, dwelt in Padan-aram, and received Jacob his sister's son when he fled from Esau (Genesis 24:29; 28:5; 29:13-14). He beguiled Jacob, giving him Leah for Rachel after seven years' service, and changed his wages ten times, but God suffered him not to hurt Jacob (Genesis 29:21-27; 31:7). Pursuing Jacob to mount Gilead, he was warned of God in a dream by night, and they made a covenant at the heap of witness called Mizpah: The LORD watch between me and thee (Genesis 31:22-55).
LION: The lion in scripture is the mightiest among beasts, which turneth not away for any (Proverbs 30:30). Samson tore a lion as a kid, David slew both a lion and a bear that took lambs of his flock, and Benaiah slew a lion in a pit in time of snow (Judges 14:5-6; 1 Samuel 17:34-36; 2 Samuel 23:20). Daniel was delivered out of the mouths of lions, and the scripture likens the devil to a roaring lion seeking whom he may devour, while Christ is the Lion of the tribe of Juda who hath prevailed (Daniel 6:22; 1 Peter 5:8; Revelation 5:5).
LOT, THE: The lot was cast into the lap to determine matters, but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD; the lot causeth contentions to cease (Proverbs 16:33; 18:18). By lot the land of Canaan was divided to the tribes, the scapegoat was chosen, the courses of priests were ordered, and Achan and Jonathan were discovered (Numbers 26:55; Leviticus 16:8; 1 Chronicles 24:5; Joshua 7:14-18; 1 Samuel 14:41-42). The soldiers parted the Lord's garments, casting lots, that the scripture might be fulfilled, and the apostles gave forth lots in choosing Matthias (Matthew 27:35; Psalm 22:18; Acts 1:26).
LOT: Lot, the son of Haran, went with his uncle Abram from Ur and out of Egypt, until their substance was so great they could not dwell together; Lot chose all the plain of Jordan and pitched his tent toward Sodom (Genesis 11:31; 13:5-12). He was carried captive and rescued by Abram, and when the LORD destroyed Sodom, two angels brought him out, for God remembered Abraham; his wife looked back and became a pillar of salt (Genesis 14:12-16; 19:15-26). He is called just Lot, whose righteous soul was vexed from day to day with the unlawful deeds of the wicked; his daughters bare Moab and Ben-ammi, fathers of the Moabites and Ammonites (2 Peter 2:7-8; Genesis 19:30-38).
MAACHAH: Maachah was a name borne by several persons and a small kingdom. Maachah the daughter of Talmai king of Geshur was David's wife and the mother of Absalom (2 Samuel 3:3). Maachah the mother of king Asa was removed from being queen, because she had made an idol in a grove, and Asa destroyed her idol and burnt it by the brook Kidron (1 Kings 15:13). The Maachathites dwelt beside Geshur at Israel's border, and the king of Maachah aided Ammon against David (Joshua 13:13; 2 Samuel 10:6-8).
MAASEIAH: Maaseiah was a common name in Judah and Levi: Levite musicians before the ark, a captain who helped Jehoiada crown Joash, an officer under Uzziah, a king's son slain by an Ephraimite in the days of Ahaz, and a governor of Jerusalem who repaired the house of the LORD under Josiah (1 Chronicles 15:18; 2 Chronicles 23:1; 26:11; 28:7; 34:8). After the captivity, priests and others of this name put away strange wives, stood by Ezra at the reading of the law, and dwelt in Jerusalem (Ezra 10:18-30; Nehemiah 8:4-7; 11:5-7). The father of Zephaniah the priest and the keeper of the door in Jeremiah's day also bore the name (Jeremiah 21:1; 35:4).
MACHIR: Machir was the firstborn of Manasseh, the father of Gilead, whose children took Gilead and dispossessed the Amorite; Moses gave Gilead unto Machir (Genesis 50:23; Numbers 32:39-40; Joshua 17:1). His descendants were a family of war. Another Machir, the son of Ammiel of Lo-debar, nourished Mephibosheth the son of Jonathan, and brought beds, basons, and victuals to David when he fled from Absalom (2 Samuel 9:4-5; 17:27-29).
MALCHIAH: Malchiah (also written Malchijah) was the name of many men: a Gershonite Levite, priests of the courses of David, and fathers of men who served in Jeremiah's day (1 Chronicles 6:40; 24:9; Jeremiah 21:1; 38:1). Jeremiah was cast into the dungeon of Malchiah the son of Hammelech in the court of the prison (Jeremiah 38:6). After the captivity, men of this name put away strange wives, repaired portions of the wall of Jerusalem and the tower of the furnaces, stood by Ezra at the reading of the law, and sealed the covenant (Ezra 10:25-31; Nehemiah 3:11, 14, 31; 8:4; 10:3).
MANASSEH: Manasseh was the firstborn of Joseph in Egypt, whom Jacob adopted and blessed, yet set Ephraim the younger before him (Genesis 41:51; 48:5, 13-20). His tribe received inheritance on both sides Jordan (Joshua 17). Manasseh the son of Hezekiah reigned fifty and five years in Jerusalem and did evil above the heathen, shedding innocent blood very much, and making his sons pass through the fire; carried in fetters to Babylon, he humbled himself greatly before the God of his fathers, who was intreated of him and brought him again to Jerusalem, and he took away the strange gods (2 Kings 21:1-16; 2 Chronicles 33:1-16).
MARY: Several women bore this name. Mary of Nazareth, a virgin espoused to Joseph, was told by the angel Gabriel, Thou shalt bring forth a son, and shalt call his name JESUS; she answered, Behold the handmaid of the Lord, and magnified God her Saviour (Luke 1:26-38, 46-55). She brought forth her firstborn son at Bethlehem, kept these things and pondered them in her heart, stood by the cross of Jesus, and continued in prayer with the disciples after His ascension (Luke 2:7, 19; John 19:25-27; Acts 1:14). Mary Magdalene, out of whom went seven devils, was first at the sepulchre and first to see the risen Lord (Luke 8:2; John 20:1-18). Mary of Bethany sat at Jesus' feet and heard His word, choosing that good part, and anointed His feet with costly ointment (Luke 10:39-42; John 12:3).
MEGIDDO: Megiddo was a Canaanite royal city whose king Joshua smote, allotted to Manasseh, though the Canaanites would dwell in that land (Joshua 12:21; 17:11-12; Judges 1:27). By the waters of Megiddo the kings of Canaan fought and were overcome in the days of Deborah and Barak (Judges 5:19). Solomon built Megiddo; there Ahaziah king of Judah died fleeing from Jehu, and there good king Josiah was slain in battle against Pharaoh-nechoh, and the mourning of Hadadrimmon in the valley of Megiddon became a proverb (1 Kings 9:15; 2 Kings 9:27; 23:29-30; Zechariah 12:11).
MEPHIBOSHETH: Mephibosheth, the son of Jonathan, was five years old when tidings came of Saul and Jonathan; his nurse fled in haste, and he fell and became lame on his feet (2 Samuel 4:4). David sought him out for Jonathan's sake, restored to him all the land of Saul, and said, Thou shalt eat bread at my table continually (2 Samuel 9). Slandered by Ziba his servant during Absalom's rebellion, he met the returning king unwashed and unshaven, saying, Yea, let him take all, forasmuch as my lord the king is come again in peace (2 Samuel 16:1-4; 19:24-30). David spared him from the Gibeonites' vengeance because of the LORD's oath between David and Jonathan (2 Samuel 21:7).
MICAH: Micah the Morasthite prophesied in the days of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah concerning Samaria and Jerusalem (Micah 1:1). He foretold that Bethlehem Ephratah should bring forth the ruler in Israel, whose goings forth have been from of old, from everlasting, and asked, What doth the LORD require of thee, but to do justly, and to love mercy, and to walk humbly with thy God? (Micah 5:2; 6:8). An earlier Micah of mount Ephraim made a house of gods and hired a Levite for his priest, whom the Danites took away with the graven image (Judges 17-18).
MICHAIAH: Michaiah (a form of Micaiah) was the name of several persons: the father of Achbor, whom Josiah sent to inquire of the LORD concerning the book of the law; the daughter of Uriel, mother of Abijah king of Judah; and a prince whom Jehoshaphat sent to teach in the cities of Judah with the book of the law (2 Kings 22:12; 2 Chronicles 13:2; 17:7-9). Michaiah the son of Gemariah heard Baruch read the words of Jeremiah and declared them to the princes (Jeremiah 36:11-13). Others of the name appear among the priests and singers at the dedication of the wall of Jerusalem (Nehemiah 12:35, 41).
MIRIAM: Miriam the prophetess was the sister of Aaron and Moses; as a girl she watched over the ark of bulrushes and spoke to Pharaoh's daughter for the child's mother (Exodus 2:4-8; 15:20). After the crossing of the Red sea she took a timbrel, and all the women went out after her, answering, Sing ye to the LORD, for he hath triumphed gloriously (Exodus 15:20-21). She spake with Aaron against Moses because of the Ethiopian woman, and the LORD smote her with leprosy seven days until Moses cried, Heal her now, O God, I beseech thee (Numbers 12). She died and was buried in Kadesh (Numbers 20:1).
MISHAEL: Mishael the son of Uzziel, of the Kohathites, was uncle's son to Aaron, and with Elzaphan carried the bodies of Nadab and Abihu out of the camp at Moses' command (Exodus 6:22; Leviticus 10:4-5). Another Mishael was one of the three companions of Daniel, called Meshach by the prince of the eunuchs; refusing to worship the golden image, he was cast into the burning fiery furnace and came forth without the smell of fire upon him (Daniel 1:6-7; 3:13-27). A Mishael also stood at Ezra's left hand when he read the law to the people (Nehemiah 8:4).
MORDECAI: Mordecai the son of Jair, a Benjamite carried from Jerusalem in the captivity, brought up Hadassah, that is, Esther, his uncle's daughter, and charged her when she was made queen (Esther 2:5-11, 20). He revealed the conspiracy of two chamberlains against the king, refused to bow to Haman, and put on sackcloth when the decree went out to destroy the Jews, charging Esther, Who knoweth whether thou art come to the kingdom for such a time as this? (Esther 2:21-23; 3:2; 4:1, 14). Haman was hanged on the gallows he had prepared for Mordecai, who was advanced next unto king Ahasuerus, great among the Jews, seeking the wealth of his people (Esther 7:10; 10:3).
MOSES: Moses, a Levite hid three months at his birth and drawn from the river by Pharaoh's daughter, fled to Midian after slaying an Egyptian, and at the backside of the desert God called to him out of the burning bush, saying, I AM THAT I AM (Exodus 2-3). By his hand the LORD sent the plagues upon Egypt, brought Israel through the Red sea, and gave the law at Sinai, where Moses was with the LORD forty days, and his face shone (Exodus 7-14; 19-24; 34:28-35). Meek above all men upon the face of the earth, he interceded for Israel, but for speaking unadvisedly at Meribah he saw the land only from Pisgah, and the LORD buried him in a valley in Moab, no man knowing of his sepulchre (Numbers 12:3; Exodus 32:11-14; Deuteronomy 34). There arose not a prophet since in Israel like unto Moses, whom the LORD knew face to face; he appeared with Elias at the transfiguration of Christ (Deuteronomy 34:10; Matthew 17:3).
NADAB: Nadab, the firstborn of Aaron, was consecrated a priest and went up with Moses and the elders, who saw the God of Israel (Exodus 6:23; 24:1, 9-11; 28:1). With Abihu his brother he offered strange fire before the LORD, which He commanded them not, and there went out fire from the LORD and devoured them (Leviticus 10:1-2). Nadab the son of Jeroboam reigned over Israel two years, did evil, and was slain by Baasha, who destroyed all the house of Jeroboam according to the word of the LORD (1 Kings 15:25-30).
NAPHTALI: Naphtali was the sixth son of Jacob, the second of Bilhah, Rachel's maid; Rachel said, With great wrestlings have I wrestled with my sister, and I have prevailed (Genesis 30:7-8). Jacob blessed him, Naphtali is a hind let loose: he giveth goodly words, and Moses said, O Naphtali, satisfied with favour, and full with the blessing of the LORD (Genesis 49:21; Deuteronomy 33:23). His tribe's portion lay in the north; Barak of Kedesh-naphtali led its men against Sisera, and in the land of Naphtali by the way of the sea the people which sat in darkness saw great light when Jesus dwelt in Capernaum (Joshua 19:32-39; Judges 4:6-10; Matthew 4:13-16).
NATHAN: Nathan the prophet stood before David: he first approved the building of the house of the LORD, then by the word of the LORD declared that David's son should build it, and that David's throne should be established for ever (2 Samuel 7:1-17). He rebuked David for his sin with the parable of the ewe lamb, saying, Thou art the man, and David said, I have sinned against the LORD (2 Samuel 12:1-13). He named Solomon Jedidiah, and with Zadok the priest anointed Solomon king at Gihon, defeating the usurpation of Adonijah (2 Samuel 12:25; 1 Kings 1). Another Nathan was a son of David born in Jerusalem, through whom the line of Mary is traced (2 Samuel 5:14; Luke 3:31).
NEBUCHADNEZZAR: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon besieged Jerusalem, carried Judah captive, burnt the house of the LORD, and brought the vessels of the temple to Babylon (2 Kings 24-25; Daniel 1:1-2). God gave him dreams of a great image and of a tree cut down, which Daniel interpreted; he set up a golden image and cast the three Hebrews into the furnace, but confessed, There is no God that can deliver after this sort (Daniel 2-3). Because his heart was lifted up, he was driven from men and did eat grass as oxen until seven times passed over him; then his reason returned, and he praised and honoured the King of heaven, whose works are truth, and those that walk in pride he is able to abase (Daniel 4).
NEHEMIAH: Nehemiah the son of Hachaliah, cupbearer to king Artaxerxes at Shushan, wept and prayed when he heard that the wall of Jerusalem was broken down, and obtained leave of the king to build it (Nehemiah 1-2). Despite the scorn and conspiracy of Sanballat, Tobiah, and Geshem, the wall was finished in fifty and two days, the builders each with his sword girded by his side, for the people had a mind to work (Nehemiah 4:6, 17-18; 6:15-16). As governor he relieved the oppression of the poor, took not the bread of the governor, gathered the people to the reading of the law, and reformed the sabbath, the tithes, and the mixed marriages, praying, Remember me, O my God, for good (Nehemiah 5; 8; 13).
NOAH: Noah, the son of Lamech, was a just man and perfect in his generations, and Noah walked with God; he found grace in the eyes of the LORD when all flesh had corrupted his way (Genesis 6:8-9, 12). At God's command he built an ark of gopher wood and entered it with his wife, his three sons and their wives, and of every living thing two of every sort, and the flood destroyed all flesh outside (Genesis 6:14-22; 7). He builded an altar when he came forth, and God blessed him and set the bow in the cloud for a token of the covenant that the waters should no more become a flood to destroy all flesh (Genesis 8:20-22; 9:8-17). A preacher of righteousness, he lived nine hundred and fifty years (2 Peter 2:5; Genesis 9:29).
OMRI: Omri, captain of the host of Israel, was made king by the people in the camp when Zimri slew Elah, and he prevailed against Tibni the son of Ginath (1 Kings 16:16-22). He bought the hill Samaria of Shemer for two talents of silver and built thereon the city of Samaria, which became the capital of Israel (1 Kings 16:24). He wrought evil in the eyes of the LORD, and did worse than all that were before him; the statutes of Omri were remembered by the prophet as a reproach, and his son Ahab reigned in his stead (1 Kings 16:25-28; Micah 6:16).
OPHIR: Ophir was a son of Joktan, of the line of Shem, and the name of the far country famed for its gold (Genesis 10:26-29). The navy of Hiram and the servants of Solomon fetched from Ophir four hundred and twenty talents of gold, with great plenty of almug trees and precious stones (1 Kings 9:26-28; 10:11). The gold of Ophir became a proverb of preciousness: Job speaks of it, the psalmist sets the queen in gold of Ophir, and Jehoshaphat's ships made to go to Ophir were broken at Ezion-geber (Job 28:16; Psalm 45:9; 1 Kings 22:48).
PASHUR: Pashur the son of Immer, priest and chief governor in the house of the LORD, smote Jeremiah the prophet and put him in the stocks; Jeremiah declared, The LORD hath not called thy name Pashur, but Magor-missabib, for he should be a terror to himself and be carried to Babylon (Jeremiah 20:1-6). Another Pashur, the son of Melchiah, was sent by king Zedekiah to inquire of Jeremiah, and was among the princes who cast the prophet into the dungeon (Jeremiah 21:1-2; 38:1-6). Sons of Pashur are numbered among the priests who returned from the captivity, of whom some had taken strange wives (Ezra 2:38; 10:22).
PAUL: Saul of Tarsus, a Pharisee of the tribe of Benjamin brought up at the feet of Gamaliel, persecuted the church, consenting unto Stephen's death (Acts 8:1-3; 22:3; Philippians 3:5). On the road to Damascus a light from heaven shined round about him, and the Lord said, Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me? — and he became a chosen vessel to bear Christ's name before the Gentiles (Acts 9:1-16). Called Paul, he preached from Jerusalem round about unto Illyricum through three missionary journeys, was stoned, beaten, shipwrecked, and imprisoned, and wrote epistles to the churches, testifying, By the grace of God I am what I am (Acts 13-28; 2 Corinthians 11:23-28; 1 Corinthians 15:10). At the end he wrote, I have fought a good fight, I have finished my course, I have kept the faith (2 Timothy 4:7).
PELATIAH: Pelatiah was the name of several men: a son of Hananiah of the line of Zerubbabel, a captain of the sons of Simeon who smote the Amalekites in mount Seir, and one who sealed the covenant in Nehemiah's day (1 Chronicles 3:21; 4:42-43; Nehemiah 10:22). Pelatiah the son of Benaiah was a prince of the people whom Ezekiel saw in vision devising mischief at the east gate of the LORD's house; as Ezekiel prophesied, Pelatiah died, and the prophet fell down and cried, Ah Lord GOD! wilt thou make a full end of the remnant of Israel? (Ezekiel 11:1-13).
PETER: Simon Bar-jona, a fisherman of Bethsaida, was brought to Jesus by his brother Andrew, and the Lord surnamed him Cephas, a stone (John 1:40-42; Matthew 4:18-20). Foremost of the twelve, he walked on the water to Jesus, confessed, Thou art the Christ, the Son of the living God, and received the keys of the kingdom of heaven; yet he denied his Lord thrice before the cock crew, and went out and wept bitterly (Matthew 14:28-31; 16:16-19; 26:69-75). Restored by the risen Christ, who charged him, Feed my sheep, he preached at Pentecost when three thousand souls were added, opened the door of faith to the Gentiles at the house of Cornelius, and wrote two epistles to the scattered strangers (John 21:15-17; Acts 2; 10; 1 Peter 1:1).
PHARAOH: Pharaoh was the title of the kings of Egypt throughout the scriptures. One Pharaoh took Sarai into his house and was plagued; another exalted Joseph over all Egypt at the interpreting of his dreams; a later Pharaoh which knew not Joseph made Israel to serve with rigour and commanded their sons cast into the river (Genesis 12:15-20; 41; Exodus 1:8-22). The Pharaoh of the exodus hardened his heart under ten plagues, and his host was drowned in the Red sea, for the LORD had said, In very deed for this cause have I raised thee up, for to shew in thee my power (Exodus 5-14; 9:16; Romans 9:17). Later Pharaohs include the father of Solomon's wife, Shishak who spoiled the temple, Pharaoh-nechoh who slew Josiah, and Pharaoh-hophra of Jeremiah's prophecy (1 Kings 3:1; 14:25-26; 2 Kings 23:29; Jeremiah 44:30).
PHILIP: Philip of Bethsaida, the city of Andrew and Peter, was found by Jesus, who saith unto him, Follow me; and Philip findeth Nathanael, saying, We have found him, of whom Moses in the law, and the prophets, did write (John 1:43-45). One of the twelve, he was proved by the Lord concerning the loaves, brought the Greeks who would see Jesus, and said at supper, Lord, shew us the Father, and it sufficeth us (John 6:5-7; 12:20-22; 14:8-9). Philip the evangelist, one of the seven, preached Christ in Samaria with great joy, was sent to the desert road to Gaza where he baptized the Ethiopian eunuch, and had four daughters, virgins, which did prophesy (Acts 6:5; 8:5-40; 21:8-9).
QUEEN: The scriptures speak of queens both good and evil. The queen of Sheba came from the uttermost parts of the earth to prove Solomon with hard questions, and there was no more spirit in her; Jesus said she shall rise up in the judgment with this generation (1 Kings 10:1-13; Matthew 12:42). Esther the Jewess was made queen of Persia instead of Vashti, and by her petition saved her people from destruction (Esther 2:17; 7:3-6). Athaliah usurped Judah's throne and destroyed the seed royal, and wicked queens such as Jezebel wrought evil, while Judah in apostasy burned incense to the queen of heaven (2 Kings 11:1-3; 1 Kings 21:25; Jeremiah 44:17-25).
RABBAH: Rabbah was the royal city of the children of Ammon, where the bedstead of Og king of Bashan remained (Deuteronomy 3:11). Joab besieged Rabbah while David tarried at Jerusalem — the siege in which Uriah the Hittite was slain — and took the city of waters, and David took the king's crown of a talent of gold (2 Samuel 11:1; 12:26-31). The prophets pronounced judgment against Rabbah of the Ammonites, that it should be a desolate heap and a stable for camels (Jeremiah 49:2-3; Ezekiel 25:5; Amos 1:14).
RACHEL: Rachel, the younger daughter of Laban, was beautiful and well favoured, and Jacob served seven years for her, and they seemed unto him but a few days, for the love he had to her (Genesis 29:16-20). Long barren while her sister bare sons, she cried, Give me children, or else I die; and God remembered Rachel and she bare Joseph (Genesis 30:1, 22-24). She died in travail with Benjamin by the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem, and Jacob set a pillar upon her grave; the prophet spake of Rahel weeping for her children, fulfilled when Herod slew the children of Bethlehem (Genesis 35:16-20; Jeremiah 31:15; Matthew 2:16-18).
RAHAB: Rahab the harlot of Jericho received the two spies of Israel, hid them with the stalks of flax upon the roof, and confessed, The LORD your God, he is God in heaven above, and in earth beneath (Joshua 2:1-11). By the token of the line of scarlet thread in her window, she and all her father's household were saved alive when Jericho fell, and she dwelt in Israel (Joshua 2:18-21; 6:22-25). By faith the harlot Rahab perished not with them that believed not; she was justified by works when she had received the messengers, and she is named in the genealogy of Jesus Christ (Hebrews 11:31; James 2:25; Matthew 1:5).
RAM: Ram, the son of Hezron of the tribe of Judah, begat Amminadab, and stands in the royal line from Pharez to Boaz, to Jesse, to David; the same is called Aram in the genealogy of Jesus Christ (Ruth 4:18-22; 1 Chronicles 2:9-10; Matthew 1:3-4). The name was also borne by a son of Jerahmeel, and Elihu the Buzite, who answered Job, was of the kindred of Ram (1 Chronicles 2:25, 27; Job 32:2).
RAMOTH: Ramoth was the name of more than one place in Israel: Ramoth in Gilead, a city of refuge for the slayer, given to the Levites out of the tribe of Gad, and Ramoth of the south, whither David sent of the spoil of Ziklag (Deuteronomy 4:43; Joshua 20:8; 21:38; 1 Samuel 30:27). Among the sons of Bani who had taken strange wives after the captivity was also a man named Ramoth (Ezra 10:29).
RAMOTH-GILEAD: Ramoth-gilead was a city of refuge east of Jordan in the tribe of Gad, the seat of one of Solomon's officers (Joshua 20:8; 1 Kings 4:13). Ahab, going up with Jehoshaphat to recover it from Syria against the warning of Micaiah the prophet, was slain there by a bow drawn at a venture (1 Kings 22:1-37). There Joram was wounded warring against Hazael, and there Elisha's messenger anointed Jehu king over Israel to cut off the house of Ahab (2 Kings 8:28-29; 9:1-13).
REBEKAH (REBECCA): Rebekah, the daughter of Bethuel and sister of Laban, was found at the well by Abraham's servant, to whom she gave drink and drew water for his camels, the token that the LORD had appointed her for Isaac (Genesis 24). Isaac loved her, and being barren, he intreated the LORD for her, and she bare the twins Esau and Jacob, having been told, The elder shall serve the younger (Genesis 24:67; 25:21-26). She loved Jacob, and contrived the deceiving of Isaac for the blessing, then sent Jacob away to her brother Laban; she was buried in the cave of Machpelah (Genesis 27; 49:31).
REHOB: Rehob was the name of places and of men. The spies searched the land unto Rehob, as men come to Hamath; cities of this name were in the lot of Asher, one given to the Levites, whose Canaanite inhabitants were not driven out (Numbers 13:21; Joshua 19:28-30; 21:31; Judges 1:31). Rehob was also the father of Hadadezer king of Zobah, whom David smote, and a Levite of this name sealed the covenant in Nehemiah's day (2 Samuel 8:3, 12; Nehemiah 10:11).
REUBEN: Reuben, the firstborn of Jacob by Leah, lay with Bilhah his father's concubine, and his birthright was given unto the sons of Joseph (Genesis 29:32; 35:22; 1 Chronicles 5:1-2). He would have delivered Joseph out of his brethren's hands, saying, Shed no blood, and rent his clothes when he found him not in the pit; he offered his own two sons as surety for Benjamin (Genesis 37:21-30; 42:37). Jacob pronounced, Unstable as water, thou shalt not excel; his tribe took its inheritance east of Jordan, with much cattle (Genesis 49:3-4; Numbers 32).
RIMMON: Rimmon was a name borne by places, a man, and an idol. Rimmon the Beerothite was the father of Rechab and Baanah, the murderers of Ish-bosheth (2 Samuel 4:2-9). The rock Rimmon was the refuge whither six hundred Benjamites fled and abode four months (Judges 20:47). Rimmon was also a city in the south of Judah given to Simeon, a Levitical city in Zebulun, and the house of Rimmon was the temple of the Syrian god wherein Naaman had bowed himself (Joshua 15:32; 19:13; 1 Chronicles 6:77; 2 Kings 5:18).
RUTH: Ruth the Moabitess, widow of Mahlon, clave unto Naomi her mother in law, saying, Whither thou goest, I will go; thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God (Ruth 1:4, 14-17). She gleaned in the fields of Boaz at Bethlehem, who showed her grace, for it had been fully shewed him all that she had done for her mother in law, and how she was come to trust under the wings of the LORD God of Israel (Ruth 2). Boaz took her to wife as kinsman redeemer, and she bare Obed, the father of Jesse, the father of David, and her name stands in the genealogy of Jesus Christ (Ruth 4:9-22; Matthew 1:5).
SAMSON: Samson, the son of Manoah of the tribe of Dan, was a Nazarite unto God from the womb, born of a barren woman to whom the angel of the LORD appeared, and he began to deliver Israel out of the hand of the Philistines (Judges 13). The Spirit of the LORD came mightily upon him: he rent a lion as a kid, slew a thousand men with the jawbone of an ass, and carried away the gates of Gaza (Judges 14:5-6; 15:14-16; 16:3). Betrayed by Delilah, shaven, blinded, and bound in fetters of brass, he called on the LORD at the feast of Dagon and bowed himself with all his might, so the dead which he slew at his death were more than they which he slew in his life (Judges 16). He judged Israel twenty years and is named among the faithful in Hebrews (Judges 16:31; Hebrews 11:32).
SAMUEL: Samuel was born to Hannah in answer to prayer and lent to the LORD all the days of his life, ministering before Eli at Shiloh (1 Samuel 1). The LORD called to the child by night, and Samuel grew, and the LORD was with him, and did let none of his words fall to the ground; all Israel knew that Samuel was established to be a prophet of the LORD (1 Samuel 3). He judged Israel all his days, and at God's word anointed both Saul and David to be kings (1 Samuel 7:15; 10:1; 16:13). He testified, To obey is better than sacrifice, and, God forbid that I should sin against the LORD in ceasing to pray for you; all Israel lamented him at his death (1 Samuel 15:22; 12:23; 25:1).
SARAH: Sarai, the wife of Abram, was barren, and gave Hagar her handmaid to her husband; God changed her name to Sarah, saying, She shall be a mother of nations (Genesis 16:1-3; 17:15-16). She laughed within herself at the promise of a son in her old age, but through faith received strength to conceive seed, and bare Isaac at ninety years old, saying, God hath made me to laugh (Genesis 18:12; Hebrews 11:11; Genesis 21:1-7). She died at an hundred and twenty and seven years in Hebron, and was buried in the cave of Machpelah; the scripture holds her forth as an example of holy women, whose daughters ye are as long as ye do well (Genesis 23; 1 Peter 3:5-6).
SAUL: Saul the son of Kish, a Benjamite, a choice young man and goodly, higher than any of the people from his shoulders upward, was anointed by Samuel as the first king of Israel (1 Samuel 9:1-2; 10:1, 23-24). He delivered Jabesh-gilead and smote the Philistines, but forced himself to offer a burnt offering and spared Agag and the best of the flock, and Samuel told him, Because thou hast rejected the word of the LORD, he hath also rejected thee from being king (1 Samuel 11; 13:8-14; 15:22-23). An evil spirit troubled him, and he pursued David in the wilderness for years, though David twice spared his life; at last he consulted a woman with a familiar spirit at Endor (1 Samuel 16:14; 24; 26; 28). Wounded by archers on mount Gilboa, he fell upon his own sword, and Israel's beauty was slain upon the high places (1 Samuel 31; 2 Samuel 1:19).
SERAIAH: Seraiah was borne by many men: David's scribe, the son of Kenaz of Judah, and a prince of Simeon (2 Samuel 8:17; 1 Chronicles 4:13-14, 35). Seraiah the chief priest was taken at the fall of Jerusalem and slain by the king of Babylon at Riblah; he was the father of Jehozadak, who went into captivity, and an ancestor of Ezra the scribe (2 Kings 25:18-21; 1 Chronicles 6:14; Ezra 7:1). Another Seraiah, the son of Neriah, a quiet prince, carried Jeremiah's book to Babylon and read it, casting it bound to a stone into Euphrates for a sign (Jeremiah 51:59-64). Others of this name returned with Zerubbabel and sealed the covenant (Ezra 2:2; Nehemiah 10:2).
SHALLUM: Shallum was a name borne by many.
