Shallum the son of Jabesh conspired against Zachariah king of Israel, smote him before the people, and reigned a full month in Samaria before Menahem slew him (2 Kings 15:10-14). Shallum the son of Josiah, also called Jehoahaz, reigned in his father's stead and was carried away, to return no more (Jeremiah 22:11-12; 2 Kings 23:30-34). Others include the husband of Huldah the prophetess, keepers of the gates among the Levites, the uncle of Jeremiah whose field the prophet bought, and men who repaired the wall and put away strange wives (2 Kings 22:14; 1 Chronicles 9:17-19; Jeremiah 32:7; Nehemiah 3:12; Ezra 10:24, 42).
SHAMMAH: Shammah was the name of several men: a duke of Edom, the son of Reuel; the third son of Jesse, who followed Saul to the battle against Goliath; and mighty men of David (Genesis 36:13, 17; 1 Samuel 17:13). Shammah the son of Agee the Hararite, one of David's three mightiest, stood in the midst of a piece of ground full of lentiles when the people fled, and defended it, and slew the Philistines; and the LORD wrought a great victory (2 Samuel 23:11-12). Another Shammah the Harodite was among the thirty (2 Samuel 23:25).
SHEBA: Sheba was the name of men, a people, and a city. The queen of Sheba came to prove Solomon with hard questions, bringing gold and spices in very great abundance, and blessed the LORD his God (1 Kings 10:1-13). Sheba the son of Bichri, a man of Belial, blew a trumpet and drew Israel away from David; besieged in Abel, his head was thrown over the wall at the word of a wise woman (2 Samuel 20). Sons of this name descend from Cush, Joktan, and Abraham by Keturah, and the prophets speak of the merchants and gold of Sheba (Genesis 10:7, 28; 25:3; Psalm 72:15; Isaiah 60:6; Ezekiel 27:22).
SHECHANIAH: Shechaniah was the name of several men: a priest of the tenth course appointed in David's day, a descendant of the royal line of David after the captivity, and priests and fathers whose sons returned with Ezra (1 Chronicles 24:11; 3:21-22; Ezra 8:3, 5). Shechaniah the son of Jehiel answered Ezra concerning the strange wives, saying, We have trespassed against our God, yet now there is hope in Israel concerning this thing, and stirred him to the covenant of reformation (Ezra 10:2-4). Another was the father in law of Tobiah the Ammonite, and a priest of this name went up with Zerubbabel (Nehemiah 6:18; 12:3).
SHECHEM: Shechem was the son of Hamor the Hivite, prince of the country, who defiled Dinah the daughter of Jacob, and was slain with all the males of his city by Simeon and Levi in their deceit and anger (Genesis 34). The city of Shechem, in mount Ephraim, was where the LORD first appeared to Abram in the land, where Jacob buried the strange gods under an oak, and where Joseph's bones were buried (Genesis 12:6-7; 35:4; Joshua 24:32). There Joshua made his farewell covenant with Israel, there Abimelech the son of Gideon was made king and the city afterward destroyed, and there Rehoboam came to be made king when the kingdom was rent (Joshua 24:1, 25; Judges 9; 1 Kings 12:1-19). It was a city of refuge (Joshua 20:7).
SHELEMIAH: Shelemiah was the name of several men: a porter chosen by lot for the east gate in David's day, and ancestors of officers in the days of Jeremiah, including the father of Jehucal and the father of Irijah, who took Jeremiah as one falling away to the Chaldeans (1 Chronicles 26:14; Jeremiah 37:3, 13). The son of Abdeel of this name was commanded to take Baruch and Jeremiah, but the LORD hid them (Jeremiah 36:26). After the captivity, sons of Bani of this name put away strange wives, the father of Hananiah repaired part of the wall, and Nehemiah made Shelemiah the priest a treasurer over the treasuries, for he was counted faithful (Ezra 10:39, 41; Nehemiah 3:30; 13:13).
SHELOMITH: Shelomith was a name borne by both men and women. Shelomith the daughter of Dibri, of the tribe of Dan, was the mother of the man who blasphemed the name of the LORD in the camp and was stoned (Leviticus 24:10-14). Others of the name include a daughter of Zerubbabel, chief Levites of the sons of Izhar and of the sons of Eliezer who were over the dedicated treasures, a child of Rehoboam, and the sons of Shelomith who returned with Ezra (1 Chronicles 3:19; 23:18; 26:25-28; 2 Chronicles 11:20; Ezra 8:10).
SHEMAIAH: Shemaiah was among the commonest names in Israel. Shemaiah the man of God forbade Rehoboam to war against the ten tribes, saying, This thing is from me, saith the LORD; he later declared the LORD's mercy when the princes humbled themselves before Shishak's invasion (1 Kings 12:22-24; 2 Chronicles 12:5-8). Shemaiah the Nehelamite, a false prophet among the captives, wrote against Jeremiah and was judged of the LORD (Jeremiah 29:24-32). Others include Levites, porters, princes who taught the law under Jehoshaphat, a keeper of the east gate who repaired the wall, the false hireling who counselled Nehemiah to flee into the temple, and priests at the dedication of the wall (1 Chronicles 9:14; 26:4-7; 2 Chronicles 17:8; Nehemiah 3:29; 6:10-13; 12:34-36).
SHEPHATIAH: Shephatiah was the name of several men: the fifth son of David, born of Abital in Hebron; a Benjamite of Gibeah's line; a prince of Simeon; and a son of king Jehoshaphat (2 Samuel 3:4; 1 Chronicles 9:8; 27:16; 2 Chronicles 21:2). Shephatiah the son of Mattan was among the princes who heard Jeremiah's words and sought his death, casting him into the dungeon of Malchiah (Jeremiah 38:1-6). The children of Shephatiah returned from the captivity with Zerubbabel and with Ezra, and a man of his line dwelt in Jerusalem of the children of Perez (Ezra 2:4, 57; 8:8; Nehemiah 11:4).
SHIMEI: Shimei was a name borne by many, chief among them Shimei the son of Gera, a Benjamite of the house of Saul, who cursed David and cast stones at him as he fled from Absalom, crying, Come out, thou bloody man (2 Samuel 16:5-13). David sware not to put him to death when he came down repenting at Jordan, but charged Solomon concerning him; Shimei transgressed his bounds after three years, going to Gath, and Solomon commanded Benaiah, who fell upon him that he died (2 Samuel 19:16-23; 1 Kings 2:8-9, 36-46). Others include Shimei the son of Gershon the Levite and his family, a brother of David's, an officer faithful in Adonijah's day, and many who put away strange wives (Exodus 6:17; Numbers 3:18; 1 Kings 1:8; Ezra 10:23-38).
SHITTIM: Shittim, in the plains of Moab over against Jericho, was Israel's last encampment before crossing Jordan (Numbers 33:49; 25:1). There the people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab and joined themselves unto Baal-peor, and the plague was stayed by the zeal of Phinehas (Numbers 25). From Shittim Joshua sent the two spies to Jericho, and from Shittim Israel removed and passed over Jordan on dry ground (Joshua 2:1; 3:1). The prophet bade Israel remember what befell from Shittim unto Gilgal, that they might know the righteousness of the LORD, and promised a fountain to water the valley of Shittim (Micah 6:5; Joel 3:18).
SIDON: Sidon (also written Zidon) was the firstborn of Canaan, and the great city of that name on the sea was the border of the Canaanites (Genesis 10:15, 19). Great Zidon fell in the lot of Asher, but Israel drave not out its inhabitants, and the gods of Zidon became a snare; Jezebel was the daughter of the king of the Zidonians (Joshua 11:8; Judges 1:31; 10:6; 1 Kings 16:31). Elijah was sent to Zarephath, which belongeth to Zidon, to a widow woman; the prophets pronounced burdens upon Zidon, and Jesus said it shall be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon at the day of judgment than for the cities which repented not (1 Kings 17:9; Ezekiel 28:21-23; Matthew 11:21-22).
SILAS: Silas, a chief man among the brethren and a prophet, was chosen at Jerusalem to carry the decrees of the apostles to Antioch, where he exhorted and confirmed the brethren (Acts 15:22, 32). Paul chose him for his second journey; at Philippi they were beaten and thrust into the inner prison, where at midnight they prayed and sang praises unto God, and the prison doors were opened, and the jailor believed (Acts 15:40; 16:19-34). He laboured with Paul at Thessalonica, Berea, and Corinth, and as Silvanus is joined in the epistles to the Thessalonians and named a faithful brother by Peter (Acts 17-18; 1 Thessalonians 1:1; 1 Peter 5:12).
SIMEON: Simeon was the second son of Jacob by Leah, who said, Because the LORD hath heard that I was hated, he hath therefore given me this son also (Genesis 29:33). With Levi he slew the men of Shechem for the defiling of Dinah, and Jacob in blessing divided them in Jacob and scattered them in Israel; Joseph bound Simeon before his brethren in Egypt (Genesis 34:25-30; 49:5-7; 42:24). His tribe's inheritance lay within the lot of Judah (Joshua 19:1-9). Simeon of Jerusalem, just and devout, waited for the consolation of Israel, and took the child Jesus in his arms, saying, Lord, now lettest thou thy servant depart in peace, for mine eyes have seen thy salvation (Luke 2:25-35).
SODOM: Sodom was a city of the plain toward which Lot pitched his tent, whose men were wicked and sinners before the LORD exceedingly (Genesis 13:12-13). Abraham interceded for it, but ten righteous were not found, and the LORD rained upon Sodom and upon Gomorrah brimstone and fire out of heaven, overthrowing the cities of the plain (Genesis 18:23-33; 19:24-25). Sodom stands throughout scripture as the ensample of judgment: the prophets likened rebellious Israel to Sodom, and Jesus said it shall be more tolerable for the land of Sodom in the day of judgment than for them that reject His word (Isaiah 1:9-10; Ezekiel 16:46-56; Matthew 10:15; 11:23-24; Jude 1:7).
SOLOMON: Solomon, the son of David and Bath-sheba, was anointed king at Gihon, and when the LORD appeared to him at Gibeon he asked an understanding heart; God gave him wisdom exceeding all men, and riches and honour also (1 Kings 1:38-39; 3:5-13; 4:29-34). He built the house of the LORD in seven years, and at its dedication the glory of the LORD filled the house (1 Kings 6-8). He spake three thousand proverbs, and his songs were a thousand and five, and the queen of Sheba came to hear his wisdom (1 Kings 4:32; 10:1-9). But his outlandish wives turned away his heart after other gods when he was old, and the LORD rent the kingdom from his son, saving one tribe for David's sake (1 Kings 11).
TABERNACLES, FEAST OF: The feast of tabernacles was kept seven days in the seventh month, when Israel dwelt in booths, that their generations might know that the LORD made the children of Israel to dwell in booths when He brought them out of Egypt (Leviticus 23:34-43). It was the feast of ingathering at the year's end, one of the three times all males appeared before the LORD, kept with rejoicing by all households (Exodus 23:16-17; Deuteronomy 16:13-15). Solomon dedicated the temple at this feast; it was kept with great gladness in the days of Nehemiah, such as had not been since Joshua; Jesus taught in the temple at this feast, and in the last day, that great day of the feast, cried, If any man thirst, let him come unto me, and drink (1 Kings 8:2, 65; Nehemiah 8:14-18; John 7:2, 14, 37). Zechariah prophesied all nations shall go up to keep it (Zechariah 14:16).
TARSHISH: Tarshish was a son of Javan, and the name of a far country of ships and merchandise (Genesis 10:4). The ships of Tarshish brought Solomon gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks once in three years, and the navy of Jehoshaphat made to go to Tarshish was broken at Ezion-geber (1 Kings 10:22; 2 Chronicles 20:36-37). Jonah rose up to flee unto Tarshish from the presence of the LORD (Jonah 1:3). The prophets speak of the ships and kings of Tarshish: the kings of Tarshish and of the isles shall bring presents, and the ships of Tarshish first, to bring thy sons from far (Psalm 72:10; Isaiah 60:9; 23:1).
THOMAS: Thomas, called Didymus, was one of the twelve, who said when Jesus would go again into Judaea, Let us also go, that we may die with him (Matthew 10:3; John 11:16). At the supper he asked, Lord, we know not whither thou goest; and how can we know the way? — and Jesus answered, I am the way, the truth, and the life (John 14:5-6). Absent when the risen Lord first appeared, he said, Except I shall see in his hands the print of the nails, I will not believe; eight days after, Jesus stood in the midst and bade him reach hither his finger, and Thomas answered, My Lord and my God (John 20:24-29).
TIMOTHY: Timotheus of Lystra, the son of a Jewess which believed and a Greek father, was well reported of by the brethren, and Paul took him forth on his journeys (Acts 16:1-3). From a child he had known the holy scriptures, taught by the unfeigned faith that dwelt first in his grandmother Lois and his mother Eunice (2 Timothy 1:5; 3:15). Paul's own son in the faith, he served with him in the gospel as a son with the father, was sent to Thessalonica, Corinth, and Ephesus, and received the two epistles bearing his name, being charged, Let no man despise thy youth, and, Preach the word (1 Timothy 1:2; Philippians 2:19-22; 1 Timothy 4:12; 2 Timothy 4:2).
TIRZAH: Tirzah was one of the five daughters of Zelophehad, who came before Moses asking an inheritance among their father's brethren, and the LORD said, The daughters of Zelophehad speak right (Numbers 26:33; 27:1-7). Tirzah was also a Canaanite royal city taken by Joshua, which became the seat of the kings of Israel from Jeroboam until Omri built Samaria; there Baasha reigned and was buried, and Zimri burnt the king's house over himself (Joshua 12:24; 1 Kings 14:17; 15:33; 16:8-18, 23-24). Its beauty was proverbial: Thou art beautiful, O my love, as Tirzah (Song of Solomon 6:4).
TITUS: Titus was a Greek whom Paul took up to Jerusalem, and he was not compelled to be circumcised (Galatians 2:1-3). Paul's own son after the common faith and his partner and fellowhelper, he carried the apostle's letter to Corinth, where his coming and his report comforted Paul, and he forwarded the collection for the saints (Titus 1:4; 2 Corinthians 7:6-7, 13-15; 8:6, 16-23). Paul left him in Crete to set in order the things that are wanting, and ordain elders in every city, and wrote him the epistle that bears his name (Titus 1:5).
URIAH: Uriah the Hittite, the husband of Bath-sheba, was one of David's mighty men (2 Samuel 23:39). Recalled from the siege of Rabbah, he would not go down to his house, saying, The ark, and Israel, and Judah, abide in tents; shall I then go into mine house? (2 Samuel 11:6-11). David wrote to Joab, Set ye Uriah in the forefront of the hottest battle, and he was slain; the thing that David had done displeased the LORD, and Nathan the prophet declared, Thou hast killed Uriah the Hittite with the sword of the children of Ammon (2 Samuel 11:14-17; 12:9). Uriah the priest in Ahaz's day built the altar after the pattern of Damascus, and a Urijah stood by Ezra at the reading of the law (2 Kings 16:10-16; Nehemiah 8:4).
URIJAH: Urijah the son of Shemaiah of Kirjath-jearim prophesied against Jerusalem and against Judah according to all the words of Jeremiah (Jeremiah 26:20). When Jehoiakim the king sought to put him to death, he fled into Egypt, but the king fetched him thence and slew him with the sword, and cast his dead body into the graves of the common people (Jeremiah 26:21-23). The name is also written of Uriah the priest who made the altar for Ahaz, and of priests in the days of Ezra and Nehemiah (2 Kings 16:10-11; Nehemiah 3:4; 8:4).
ZEBULUN: Zebulun was the tenth son of Jacob, the sixth of Leah, who said, Now will my husband dwell with me; and Jacob prophesied, Zebulun shall dwell at the haven of the sea, and he shall be for an haven of ships (Genesis 30:19-20; 49:13). Moses blessed him, Rejoice, Zebulun, in thy going out; his tribe jeoparded their lives unto the death in the high places of the field with Barak against Sisera (Deuteronomy 33:18-19; Judges 5:18). In the land of Zabulon and Nephthalim, by the way of the sea, the people which sat in darkness saw great light, as Esaias had spoken (Isaiah 9:1-2; Matthew 4:13-16).
ZECHARIAH (ZECHARIAS): Zechariah the son of Berechiah, the son of Iddo the prophet, prophesied with Haggai to the Jews that were come up from the captivity, stirring them to build the house of God (Ezra 5:1-2; Zechariah 1:1). He received night visions of horns and carpenters, of Joshua the high priest, of the golden candlestick, and of the flying roll, and foretold the King coming lowly, riding upon an ass, the thirty pieces of silver, and the fountain opened for sin (Zechariah 1-6; 9:9; 11:12-13; 13:1). Another Zechariah, son of Jehoiada the priest, was stoned in the court of the house of the LORD at the commandment of king Joash, saying as he died, The LORD look upon it, and require it (2 Chronicles 24:20-22). Zacharias the priest, father of John the Baptist, was struck dumb for unbelief and prophesied, being filled with the Holy Ghost, when his mouth was opened (Luke 1).
ZEPHANIAH: Zephaniah the son of Cushi, of the line of Hizkiah, prophesied in the days of Josiah king of Judah, declaring the great day of the LORD, a day of wrath, a day of trouble and distress, and calling, Seek ye the LORD, all ye meek of the earth; it may be ye shall be hid in the day of the LORD's anger (Zephaniah 1:1, 14-15; 2:3). He foretold judgment on the nations and the restoration of a pure language to the people, when the King of Israel, even the LORD, is in the midst of thee, joying over thee with singing (Zephaniah 3:9, 15-17). Zephaniah the second priest in Zedekiah's day carried messages between the king and Jeremiah, and was slain by the king of Babylon at Riblah (Jeremiah 21:1; 29:25-29; 2 Kings 25:18-21).
ZERUBBABEL: Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, of the royal seed of David, led the first return of the captivity to Jerusalem with Jeshua the priest, builded the altar, and laid the foundation of the temple with weeping and shouting mingled (Ezra 2:2; 3:2-13). Stirred up by Haggai and Zechariah when the work ceased, he finished the house, according to the word, Not by might, nor by power, but by my spirit, saith the LORD of hosts; the hands of Zerubbabel have laid the foundation of this house; his hands shall also finish it (Ezra 5:1-2; 6:14-15; Zechariah 4:6-9). The LORD called him my servant, and said, I will make thee as a signet; he stands in the genealogy of Jesus Christ (Haggai 2:23; Matthew 1:12-13).
ZOBAH: Zobah was a kingdom of Syria against which Saul warred, and whose king Hadadezer the son of Rehob was twice smitten by David, who took from him a thousand chariots and much brass, dedicating the spoil unto the LORD (1 Samuel 14:47; 2 Samuel 8:3-12). The Ammonites hired the Syrians of Zobah against David, and Joab and Abishai put them to flight (2 Samuel 10:6-14). Rezon the son of Eliadah, which fled from his lord Hadadezer king of Zobah, became an adversary to Israel all the days of Solomon (1 Kings 11:23-25).
ZORAH: Zorah was a city in the lowland, numbered to Judah and to Dan, the home of Manoah the Danite (Joshua 15:33; 19:41; Judges 13:2). There the angel of the LORD appeared to Manoah's barren wife, and Samson was born, and the Spirit of the LORD began to move him at times in the camp of Dan between Zorah and Eshtaol; between Zorah and Eshtaol he was buried in the buryingplace of Manoah his father (Judges 13; 16:31). From Zorah the Danites sent men to spy out the land, and Rehoboam built it for defence in Judah (Judges 18:2; 2 Chronicles 11:5-10).
